items.title: Why was semaglutide engineered to be a once-weekly subcutaneous injection?

items.reference: WHY Q7: "rapid, pronounced reduction in blood glucose... is associated with a temporary ischemia-driven worsening" (Paper 6, Paper 43)

items.question: ESSENCE Part 1 demonstrated that semaglutide 2.4 mg in MASH with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis achieved which landmark hepatic outcome beyond steatohepatitis resolution?
options: Complete cirrhosis reversal in all patients
Statistically significant regression of liver fibrosis
Normalization of alpha-1 antitrypsin levels
Elimination of hepatitis B co-infection
items.explanation: ESSENCE was the first Phase 3 trial showing semaglutide 2.4 mg resolves MASH and significantly regresses liver fibrosis.
items.reference: WHY Q46: "significantly resolves MASH and, crucially, achieves a statistically significant regression of liver fibrosis" (Paper 36, ESSENCE)

items.question: Oral semaglutide has average bioavailability of ~1% yet achieves therapeutic steady-state levels. What dosing strategy compensates for this?
options: Single monthly mega-dose
Daily dosing establishing stable plasma levels despite variability
Subcutaneous rescue injection with each oral dose
Continuous IV infusion
items.explanation: Despite ~1% bioavailability with high PK variability, daily oral dosing achieves stable therapeutic steady-state concentrations.
items.reference: HOW Q78: "bioavailability averages ~1%. However, daily dosing establishes highly stable, therapeutic steady-state blood levels" (Paper 37, Paper 38)

items.question: A patient with eGFR 35 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD stage 3b) requires oral semaglutide. What dose adjustment is needed?
options: Reduce to 3 mg daily
Reduce to every-other-day dosing
No dose adjustment; PK driven by proteolytic degradation not renal excretion
Contraindicated — use insulin only
items.explanation: Semaglutide clearance is primarily via systemic proteolytic degradation, not renal excretion; PIONEER 5 confirmed safety and PK in moderate renal impairment without adjustment.
items.reference: WHY Q19: "clearance of semaglutide is primarily driven by systemic metabolic peptide degradation rather than renal excretion" (Paper 16, Paper 48)

items.question: In STEP-TEENS, semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly in adolescents with obesity reduced BMI by what percentage at week 68?
items.explanation: STEP-TEENS showed a 16.1% BMI reduction at week 68 with efficacy comparable or superior to adult trials.
items.reference: HOW Q75: "reduced average BMI by 16.1% at week 68" (Paper 30, STEP-TEENS)

items.question: With chronic semaglutide use, which effect on gastric emptying is observed compared to initial dosing?
options: Progressive acceleration beyond baseline
Partial tachyphylaxis (desensitization) while central weight-loss effects persist
Complete permanent cessation of gastric motility
No effect at any time point
items.explanation: Gastric emptying delay is pronounced initially but partially attenuates with chronic use; central appetite/weight effects remain fully maintained.
items.reference: HOW Q61: "undergoes partial tachyphylaxis (desensitization) over chronic use, whereas central weight-loss effects are fully maintained" (Paper 40)

items.question: In SELECT (obesity without diabetes), semaglutide 2.4 mg reduced 3-point MACE hazard ratio to what value over ~39 months?
items.explanation: SELECT showed HR 0.80 (20% relative risk reduction) for MACE in non-diabetic obese patients.
items.reference: HOW Q74: "reduces the hazard ratio of 3-point MACE by 20% (HR 0.80)" (Paper 33, SELECT)

items.question: In FLOW, semaglutide reduced the composite renal endpoint (≥50% eGFR decline, kidney failure, or kidney-related death) by what relative amount?
items.explanation: FLOW demonstrated a 24% reduction in composite kidney disease progression events (HR 0.76).
items.reference: HOW Q95: "lowers the hazard ratio of composite renal events... by 24% (HR 0.76)" (Paper 34, FLOW)

items.question: Across major semaglutide programs (SUSTAIN, STEP, SELECT), acute pancreatitis incidence is best characterized as:
options: >5% requiring hospitalization
Significantly elevated vs all comparators
Exceptionally low (<0.1%) without significant increase vs placebo
Only seen with oral formulation
items.explanation: Pancreatitis incidence remains <0.1% across major trials with no statistically significant increase vs placebo or active comparators.
items.reference: HOW Q83: "overall incidence remains exceptionally low (<0.1% across major programs), showing no statistically significant increase" (Paper 6, Paper 33)

items.question: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) carries a class-wide GLP-1RA black box warning because:
options: Human registry data confirmed MTC increase
Rodent studies showed GLP-1–mediated thyroid C-cell hyperplasia
Semaglutide directly mutates RET proto-oncogene
SNAC accumulates in thyroid follicles
items.explanation: Rodent toxicology showed GLP-1 activation caused thyroid C-cell hyperplasia; not observed in human registries but remains a class precautionary warning.
items.reference: WHY Q44: "In rodent toxicology studies, GLP-1 activation caused thyroid C-cell hyperplasia" (Paper 6, Paper 22)

items.question: STEP 1 DXA substudy showed lean soft tissue accounted for approximately what fraction of total weight lost on semaglutide 2.4 mg?
items.explanation: Participants lost ~10.4 kg fat mass and ~6.9 kg lean mass; lean mass ~40% of total weight loss, fat mass ~60%.
items.reference: HOW Q65: "lean mass reduction accounts for approximately 40% of the total weight lost, with fat mass accounting for the remaining 60%" (Paper 22, Paper 26)

items.question: In SUSTAIN 7, once-weekly semaglutide was compared to once-weekly dulaglutide. The result for both HbA1c and weight was:
options: Non-inferior only at 0.5 mg dose
Statistically superior at both 0.5 mg and 1.0 mg semaglutide doses
Inferior for weight but superior for HbA1c
Equivalent at all doses
items.explanation: Semaglutide at both 0.5 mg and 1.0 mg was statistically superior to dulaglutide 0.75 mg and 1.5 mg for HbA1c and weight.
items.reference: HOW Q62: "statistically superior to once-weekly dulaglutide... in both HbA1c lowering and weight reduction" (Paper 7, SUSTAIN 7)

items.question: Rapid weight loss on semaglutide increases gallbladder disease risk through which mechanisms?
options: Direct cholecystokinin receptor blockade
Cholesterol mobilization from adipose plus GLP-1–mediated reduced gallbladder motility
SNAC-induced bile duct obstruction
Insulin-induced gallstone calcification
items.explanation: Rapid adipose cholesterol mobilization combined with reduced gallbladder motility from GLP-1R activation promotes biliary sludge and cholelithiasis.
items.reference: HOW Q77: "Rapid mobilization of cholesterol from adipose tissue... and GLP-1-mediated reduction in gallbladder motility" (Paper 22, Paper 33)

items.question: Semaglutide reduces appetite by acting on hypothalamic neurons to:
options: Stimulate NPY/AgRP neurons and inhibit POMC neurons
Stimulate POMC neurons and inhibit NPY/AgRP neurons
Block melanocortin-4 receptors directly
Increase ghrelin secretion from the stomach
items.explanation: Semaglutide stimulates anorexigenic POMC neurons while inhibiting orexigenic NPY/AgRP neurons in the arcuate nucleus.
items.reference: HOW Q60: "stimulates pro-opiomelanocortin (POMC) neurons... while indirectly inhibiting neuropeptide Y (NPY) and agouti-related peptide (AgRP) neurons" (Paper 39, Paper 41)

items.question: When initiating semaglutide 2.4 mg for weight management, a slow 4-week dose escalation is recommended primarily to:
options: Maximize initial weight loss in week 1
Allow GI tract adaptation and minimize nausea/vomiting/diarrhea
Achieve steady-state albumin binding faster
Prevent diabetic retinopathy
items.explanation: Gradual titration over 4 weeks allows the GI tract to adapt to GLP-1R activation, reducing severity and frequency of transient GI side effects.
items.reference: WHY Q14: "allows the gastrointestinal tract to adapt over time to GLP-1 receptor activation, minimizing... nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea" (Paper 1, Paper 22)

items.question: In PIONEER 4, oral semaglutide 14 mg daily compared to subcutaneous liraglutide 1.8 mg daily showed:
options: Inferior HbA1c reduction and inferior weight loss
Non-inferior HbA1c reduction and superior weight loss
Superior HbA1c but inferior weight loss
Equivalent outcomes on all endpoints
items.explanation: Oral semaglutide 14 mg achieved non-inferior glycemic control and statistically superior weight loss vs daily liraglutide 1.8 mg.
items.reference: HOW Q58: "achieved non-inferior HbA1c reductions and demonstrated statistically superior weight loss" (Paper 15, PIONEER 4)

items.statement: Oral semaglutide should be taken with no more than 120 mL (4 oz) of plain water after an overnight fast.
items.explanation: Excess water or food dilutes SNAC, reducing bioavailability.
items.reference: WHY Q6: "no more than 120 mL (4 oz) of plain water" (Paper 38)

items.statement: Oral semaglutide requires dose reduction in moderate chronic kidney disease (eGFR 30–59).
items.explanation: No dose adjustment needed; clearance is via proteolytic degradation, not renal excretion.
items.reference: WHY Q19: "no dose adjustment needed for oral semaglutide in patients with renal impairment" (Paper 16, Paper 48)

items.statement: STEP 4 demonstrated that obesity remission persists after semaglutide withdrawal without pharmacotherapy.
items.explanation: Patients switching to placebo rapidly regained ~two-thirds of lost weight.
items.reference: HOW Q71: "regained approximately two-thirds of their lost weight within 48 weeks" (Paper 25)

items.statement: Semaglutide monotherapy carries a high risk of symptomatic hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: Insulin secretion is glucose-dependent; hypoglycemia risk is low without insulin or sulfonylureas.
items.reference: WHY Q22: "low risk of hypoglycemia with semaglutide monotherapy" (Paper 1)

items.statement: The SELECT trial enrolled patients with obesity but without type 2 diabetes.
items.explanation: SELECT tested CV outcomes independent of glucose lowering in non-diabetic obese adults.
items.reference: WHY Q3: "individuals without type 2 diabetes" (Paper 33)

items.statement: The FLOW trial was stopped early due to efficacy on kidney outcomes.
items.explanation: Interim analysis showed overwhelming kidney-protective benefit meeting prespecified criteria.
items.reference: WHY Q4: "independent data monitoring committee recommended stopping the FLOW trial early" (Paper 34)

items.statement: Semaglutide requires dose reduction in adults ≥65 years due to altered pharmacokinetics.
items.explanation: Pooled SUSTAIN/PIONEER data show age has no clinically relevant effect on PK, efficacy, or safety.
items.reference: WHY Q28: "age has no clinically relevant effect on the pharmacokinetics" (Paper 50)

items.statement: The gastric emptying delay from semaglutide intensifies progressively over years of therapy.
items.explanation: Gastric emptying effect shows partial tachyphylaxis with chronic use.
items.reference: HOW Q61: "undergoes partial tachyphylaxis (desensitization) over chronic use" (Paper 40)

items.statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists carry a precautionary black box warning for medullary thyroid carcinoma based on rodent data.
items.explanation: Rodent C-cell hyperplasia data drives the class-wide MTC warning despite no human registry signal.
items.reference: WHY Q44: "GLP-1 activation caused thyroid C-cell hyperplasia" (Paper 6)

items.statement: Subcutaneous semaglutide pharmacokinetics differ significantly between abdominal and thigh injection sites.
items.explanation: PK profiles are equivalent across abdomen, thigh, and upper arm injection sites.
items.reference: HOW Q97: "highly equivalent and clinically interchangeable whether injected into the abdomen, thigh, or upper arm" (Paper 1)

items.statement: Semaglutide trials showed a statistically significant increase in acute pancreatitis vs placebo.
items.explanation: Incidence <0.1% with no significant increase over comparators.
items.reference: HOW Q83: "no statistically significant increase compared to placebo" (Paper 6, Paper 33)

items.statement: Semaglutide improved KCCQ symptom scores and 6-minute walk distance in obese patients with HFpEF.
items.explanation: STEP-HFpEF demonstrated improved symptoms, exercise capacity, and reduced inflammatory biomarkers.
items.reference: HOW Q64: "improves clinical symptom scores (KCCQ-CSS), increases 6-minute walk distance" (Paper 31)

items.assertion: Semaglutide can be dosed once weekly.
items.reason: A C18 fatty diacid chain promotes reversible albumin binding that protects the peptide from rapid renal clearance.
items.explanation: Both true; albumin binding extends half-life to ~1 week, enabling weekly dosing.
items.reference: WHY Q1: "extend its half-life to approximately 1 week... albumin binding" (Paper 1)

items.assertion: Oral semaglutide has very low absolute bioavailability (~1%).
items.reason: SNAC co-formulation enables transcellular gastric absorption of the peptide.
items.explanation: Both true but independent — low bioavailability reflects gut challenges; SNAC enables absorption but does not explain the low percentage itself.
items.reference: HOW Q78: "bioavailability averages ~1%" (Paper 37); WHY Q5: "SNAC acts as an absorption enhancer" (Paper 37)

items.assertion: Semaglutide 2.4 mg is used for obesity management.
items.reason: Higher doses are needed for sustained hypothalamic GLP-1 receptor activation to maximize weight loss.
items.explanation: Both true; CNS penetration for satiety requires the higher 2.4 mg dose.
items.reference: WHY Q8: "2.4 mg dose optimizes long-term hypothalamic receptor activation" (Paper 22)

items.assertion: Semaglutide can transiently worsen diabetic retinopathy in some patients.
items.reason: Semaglutide directly damages retinal capillary endothelial cells.
items.explanation: A is true (transient worsening with rapid glucose lowering); R is false (mechanism is glycemic reduction phenomenon, not direct toxicity).
items.reference: WHY Q7: "rapid, pronounced reduction in blood glucose... ischemia-driven worsening" (Paper 6)

items.assertion: Semaglutide slows chronic kidney disease progression in type 2 diabetes.
items.reason: It reduces glomerular hyperfiltration, oxidative stress, and systemic hypertension.
items.explanation: Both true; renal protection operates through hemodynamic and anti-inflammatory mechanisms.
items.reference: WHY Q18: "reduces glomerular hyperfiltration, decreases local oxidative stress" (Paper 34)

items.assertion: Obesity requires ongoing pharmacotherapy for sustained weight loss.
items.reason: STEP 4 showed patients regained most weight after switching to placebo.
items.explanation: Both true; STEP 4 withdrawal data directly supports chronic treatment need.
items.reference: WHY Q10: "rapidly regained weight, demonstrating that continuous therapy is essential" (Paper 25)

items.assertion: Nausea is a common early side effect of semaglutide.
items.reason: GLP-1R activation in the area postrema and delayed gastric emptying both contribute.
items.explanation: Both true; dual peripheral and central mechanisms explain nausea.
items.reference: WHY Q32: "delayed gastric emptying, and direct pharmacological activation of GLP-1 receptors in the area postrema" (Paper 22)

items.assertion: Semaglutide reduces MACE in obese patients without diabetes.
items.reason: Semaglutide lowers HbA1c below 6.0% in all SELECT participants.
items.explanation: A is true (SELECT showed 20% MACE reduction); R is false (SELECT enrolled non-diabetic patients — benefit is via weight loss/inflammation, not glucose lowering).
items.reference: HOW Q74: "HR 0.80" (Paper 33); WHY Q3: "without type 2 diabetes" (Paper 33)

items.assertion: Hepatic impairment does not require oral semaglutide dose adjustment.
items.reason: Semaglutide is cleared via ubiquitous proteolytic degradation, not hepatic extraction.
items.explanation: Both true; clearance pathways are redundant and not hepatic-dependent.
items.reference: WHY Q29: "cleared via universal proteolytic degradation and fatty acid oxidation pathways" (Paper 47)

items.assertion: Semaglutide reduces cravings for high-fat and sweet foods.
items.reason: It modulates the mesolimbic dopamine reward system reducing hedonic wanting.
items.explanation: Both true; reward pathway modulation explains food preference changes.
items.reference: WHY Q9: "modulates the mesolimbic dopamine reward system" (Paper 41)

items.assertion: Semaglutide is contraindicated in patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma.
items.reason: Human clinical trials confirmed increased MTC incidence with semaglutide.
items.explanation: A is true (precautionary contraindication); R is false (human MTC signal not confirmed — warning based on rodent data).
items.reference: WHY Q44: "not been observed in human clinical registries" (Paper 6)

items.assertion: Weight loss with semaglutide 2.4 mg is slightly less in type 2 diabetes than in non-diabetic obesity.
items.reason: Background insulin or sulfonylurea therapy promotes lipogenesis and counteracts weight loss.
items.explanation: Both true; STEP 2 showed ~9.6% vs ~15% loss, partly due to diabetes therapies and metabolic adaptations.
items.reference: HOW Q66: "Weight loss is slightly lower in diabetic patients (~9.6% vs ~15%)" (Paper 23)
